  B --> C{Policy Pass?}
  C -- Yes --> D[Deploy to All Clouds]
  C -- No --> E[Block & Emit Audit Logs]

questions.question: In a monorepo serving AI workloads at scale for Microsoft/Meta/HuggingFace, implement a CI gate that requires generating a Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) and performing vulnerability scans for every container image before pushing to any registry. Run Syft to generate SBOMs, plus Trivy and Grype to scan for CVEs in parallel; fail PRs if any CVE is critical or high. Include a minimal GitHub Actions workflow and sample failing/passing manifests and explain validation steps?
questions.answer: Gate design: a GitHub Actions workflow that builds the image, generates an SBOM with Syft, scans with Trivy and Grype in parallel, and fails the PR if any CVSS is critical or high; store SBOMs as arti
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Real-world security requires automated SBOMs and multi-tool vulnerability checks to prevent risky images from entering registries.

## Key Concepts
- SBOM generation with Syft
- Parallel scans with Trivy and Grype
- PR gating and artifact attachment
- Validation in CI and via PR comments

## Code Example
```yaml
# minimal GitHub Actions workflow (yaml)
name: SBOM and vulnerability gate
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]
jobs:
  gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Build image
        run: |
          docker build -t myimage:${{ github.sha }} .
      - name: Generate SBOM
        run: |
          docker run --rm -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock anchore/syft:latest DockerImage:${{ github.sha }} -o json > sbom.json
      - name: Scan with Trivy
        run: |
          docker run --rm -v /var/run/docker.sock:/var/run/docker.sock aquasecurity/trivy:latest image --exit-code 1 --no-progress myimage:${{ github.sha }} || true
      - name: Scan with Grype
        run: |
          grype myimage:${{ github.sha }} -o json > grype.json || true
      - name: Upload SBOMs
        uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3
        with:
          name: sboms
          path: sbom.json, grype.json
      - name: Fail if CVEs found
        run: |
          if [ -s grype.json ] && grep -q '"score": 7' grype.json; then exit 1; fi
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt this gate for a multi-arch build?
- How would you integrate SBOM attestation into your registry metadata?

  A[PR opened] --> B[Run SBOM + scans]
  B --> C{All pass?}
  C -- Yes --> D[Allow merge]
  C -- No --> E[Block merge and comment]

questions.answer: Implement a GitHub Actions gate that renders manifests and runs a small validator (bash+yq) to ensure every container in a PodSpec has securityContext.runAsNonRoot: true and runAsUser >= 1000, readOnl
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

## Key Concepts

questions.question: Design a CI/CD gate for a multi-cloud Kubernetes platform that enforces image provenance via Cosign/Sigstore: every container image must be signed and SBOM-attested; implement policy with Gatekeeper or Kyverno; provide minimal Policy/ConstraintTemplate, a failing manifest and a passing manifest, plus CI verification steps?
questions.answer:
Design a CI/CD gate for a multi-cloud Kubernetes platform that enforces image provenance via Cosign/Sigstore: every container image must be signed and SBOM-attested; implement policy with Gatekeeper or Kyverno; provide minimal Policy/ConstraintTemplate, a failing manifest and a passing manifest, plus CI verification steps.

**Answer:** Require every Deployment image to be Cosign-signed and SBOM-attested via Sigstore. Implement a Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate that validates the `cosign.sigstore.dev/signature` annotation and Rekor SBOM attestation before allowing the image to deploy.

```yaml
# Gatekeeper ConstraintTemplate
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8simageprovenance
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sImageProvenance
      matchLabels:
        enforce: "true"
  targets:
    - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
      rego: |
        package k8simageprovenance

        violation[msg] {
          image := input.review.object.spec.template.spec.containers[_].image
          not is_signed(image)
          msg := sprintf("Image %s is not signed with Cosign", [image])
        }

        is_signed(image) {
          input.review.object.metadata.annotations["cosign.sigstore.dev/signature"]
        }
```

**Failing Manifest:**
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
  annotations:
    # No cosign signature annotation present
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: myapp
        image: myregistry.io/myapp:v1.0.0
```

**Passing Manifest:**
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
  annotations:
    cosign.sigstore.dev/signature: "sha256:abc123..."
    sigstore.rekor: "https://rekor.sigstore.dev"
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: myapp
        image: myregistry.io/myapp:v1.0.0
```

**CI Verification Steps:**
1. Build and push the container image
2. Sign the image: `cosign sign --key cosign.key myregistry.io/myapp@v1.0.0`
3. Generate and attest the SBOM: `cosign attest --sbom --type spdx myregistry.io/myapp@v1.0.0`
4. Verify in the CI pipeline:
   ```bash
   cosign verify --key cosign.pub myregistry.io/myapp@v1.0.0
   cosign verify-attestation --key cosign.pub myregistry.io/myapp@v1.0.0
   ```
5. Gatekeeper validates annotations before admission
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates practical supply chain security controls in multi-cloud Kubernetes environments. It assesses familiarity with policy-as-code, container image signature verification, and SBOM (Software Bill of Materials) attestations integrated into CI/CD pipelines.

## Key Concepts

- **Image Provenance:** Cosign and Sigstore provide cryptographic verification that container images have not been tampered with and originate from trusted sources.
- **SBOM Attestations:** Software Bill of Materials documents enumerate software components, enabling vulnerability tracking and compliance verification.
- **Policy-as-Code:** Gatekeeper and Kyverno enforce admission control policies as code, ensuring consistent security enforcement across clusters.
- **Multi-Cloud Complexity:** Different cloud providers require unified security controls; policy engines provide a vendor-agnostic approach to image verification.

questions.question: In a Discord-scale Kubernetes platform with Helm-based deployments, implement an SBOM-driven gate that blocks deployments unless every container image has a CycloneDX SBOM in the registry and Helm charts reference SBOM-verified images. Propose a minimal GitHub Actions workflow to generate and publish SBOMs, a Kyverno policy enforcing presence at admission, and provide failing/passing manifests and testing steps?
questions.answer: Implement a deployment gate that blocks releases if any container image lacks a CycloneDX SBOM in the registry and Helm charts do not reference SBOM-verified digests. A CI workflow generates SBOMs during the build phase, publishes them alongside images in the registry, and verifies SBOM existence before deployment proceeds. Kyverno admission policies validate that all referenced images have corresponding SBOMs at admission time, rejecting any manifests that reference unverifiable images.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates real-world expertise in supply chain security by testing the ability to tie SBOMs to runtime admission policies. It aligns with vendor-agnostic security frameworks (SLSA, SBOM) and modern GitOps deployment patterns where provenance verification is critical.

## Key Concepts

- **CycloneDX SBOMs**: Standardized software bills of materials that document container contents
- **Image digests**: SHA256 references ensuring immutable image pinning
- **Kyverno admission control**: Policy engine validating deployments at Kubernetes admission time
- **Helm chart provenance**: Ensuring charts reference verified, SBOM-backed images

## Proposed Solution

### GitHub Actions Workflow: Generate and Publish SBOMs

```yaml
name: SBOM Pipeline
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  build-and-sbom:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3
      - uses: docker/login-action@v3
        with:
          registry: ghcr.io
          username: ${{ github.actor }}
          password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }}
      - uses: anchore/sbom-action@v0
        with:
          format: cyclonedx-json
          image: ${{ github.repository }}
          output-file: sbom.json
      - run: |
          echo "${{ steps.sbom.outputs.sbom-json }}" | ghcr.io/your-org/sbom-store:latest 
            --image=${{ github.repository }}@${{ steps.build.outputs.digest }}
```

### Kyverno Policy: Enforce SBOM Presence at Admission

```yaml
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-image-sbom
spec:
  validationFailureAction: Enforce
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: validate-sbom-present
    match:
      resources:
        kinds:
        - Pod
        - Deployment
        - StatefulSet
        - DaemonSet
    preconditions:
      any:
      - key: "{{ request.operation }}"
        operator: In
        value: ["CREATE", "UPDATE"]
    validate:
      message: "All container images must have an associated CycloneDX SBOM"
      foreach:
      - list: "{{ request.object.spec.template.spec.containers[] }}"
        deny:
          conditions:
          - key: "{{ element.image }}"
            operator: NotEqual
            value: ""
          - key: "{{ lookup('sbom.verify', '{{ element.image }}', 'cyclonedx') }}"
            operator: NotEqual
            value: "valid"
```

### Failing Manifest (No SBOM)

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: vulnerable-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: vulnerable-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: vulnerable-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx:1.25
```

**Expected Result**: Deployment blocked with error: "All container images must have an associated CycloneDX SBOM"

### Passing Manifest (SBOM Verified)

```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: verified-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: verified-app
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: verified-app
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: ghcr.io/your-org/app@sha256:a1b2c3d4e5f6...
        annotations:
          sbom.cyclonedx: "ghcr.io/your-org/app/sbom/a1b2c3d4e5f6"
```

**Expected Result**: Deployment allowed

## Testing Steps

1. **Apply Kyverno policy**: `kubectl apply -f kyverno-policy.yaml`
2. **Test failing manifest**: `kubectl apply -f deployment-no-sbom.yaml` — should be rejected
3. **Test passing manifest**: `kubectl apply -f deployment-with-sbom.yaml` — should be admitted
4. **Verify SBOM storage**: Confirm SBOMs are queryable via your SBOM service endpoint
5. **Audit logs**: Check Kyverno policy reports for enforcement details

In CI, scan Kubernetes manifest files (YAML) to extract image references, then run cosign verification for each image. Fail the pipeline if any image fails verification. This approach includes: (1) a minimal GitHub Actions workflow that extracts image references and verifies signatures, (2) a sample manifest with a properly signed image, (3) a failing manifest without a signature, and (4) commands to verify signatures both locally and in CI.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
This question tests practical CI policy enforcement skills using image signing—a common real-world requirement in production Kubernetes environments. It assesses understanding of supply chain security and GitOps practices.

## Key Concepts
- Container image signing with cosign
- Public key infrastructure for signature verification
- Manifest scanning in CI pipelines
- Handling signed vs. unsigned images in deployment gates
- GitOps workflow security

## Code Example

**GitHub Actions Workflow:**
```yaml
name: Verify Image Signatures
on:
  push:
    paths:
      - 'manifests/**'

jobs:
  verify:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Install cosign
        uses: sigstore/cosign-installer@v3
      - name: Extract image references
        id: images
        run: |
          echo "images=$(grep -roh 'registry.example.com/[^"]+' manifests/ | sort -u | jq -R -s -c '. | split("\n") | map(select(length > 0))')" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT
      - name: Verify signatures
        run: |
          for image in $(echo '${{ steps.images.outputs.images }}' | jq -r '.[]'); do
            cosign verify --key cosign.pub $image
          done
```

**Sample Manifest (signed image):**
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: myapp
        image: registry.example.com/myapp:v1.0.0
```

**Failing Manifest (unsigned):**
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: myapp
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: myapp
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: myapp
        image: registry.example.com/myapp:unsigned
```

## Local Verification
```bash
# Generate key pair
cosign generate-key-pair

# Sign an image
cosign sign --key cosign.key registry.example.com/myapp:v1.0.0

# Verify locally
cosign verify --key cosign.pub registry.example.com/myapp:v1.0.0
```

## CI Verification
The GitHub Actions workflow above automatically extracts all images from manifests and verifies each signature. The pipeline fails if any image lacks a valid signature, ensuring only trusted images are deployed.

  C -- Yes --> D[Deploy]
  C -- No --> E[Fail]

Rules: every Namespace must have sidecar injection and default-deny egress; each Deployment must have an AuthorizationPolicy using SPIFFE IDs; trace IDs must propagate via OpenTelemetry. Provide a minimal policy manifest, a failing permissive manifest, a passing SPIFFE-based manifest, and CI verification steps?

Design a CI gate for a multi-cloud Kubernetes mesh (AWS/GCP) that enforces Istio-based zero-trust service-to-service authentication. Requirements:
- Every Namespace must have sidecar injection and default-deny egress enabled
- Each Deployment must have an AuthorizationPolicy using SPIFFE IDs
- Trace IDs must propagate via OpenTelemetry

Provide: a minimal policy manifest, a failing permissive manifest, a passing SPIFFE-based manifest, and CI verification steps.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question validates hands-on policy-as-code experience with Istio across multi-cloud environments, assessing understanding of security posture, observability, and CI/CD integration. It confirms the candidate's ability to implement zero-trust networking principles in service mesh architectures.

## Key Concepts

- **Istio AuthorizationPolicy with SPIFFE IDs**: Cryptographically verified identities for service-to-service authentication using the Security Working Group's Architecture for Inter-Network Secure Communication (SPIFFE) standard
- **Mesh-wide sidecar injection**: Ensuring all pods have Envoy proxies for mutual TLS (mTLS) and policy enforcement
- **Default-deny egress**: Implementing network isolation by blocking all outbound traffic by default, explicitly allowing only required destinations
- **OpenTelemetry trace propagation**: Maintaining distributed tracing context across service boundaries for observability

## Evaluation Focus

Candidates should demonstrate:
- Familiarity with Istio CRDs (AuthorizationPolicy, Sidecar, PeerAuthentication)
- Understanding of SPIFFE identity format (spiffe://<trust_domain>/ns/<namespace>/sa/<service_account>)
- Knowledge of OpenTelemetry context propagation headers (b3, w3c TraceContext)
- Ability to write CI validation scripts using kubectl, istioctl, or custom validators

flowchart TD
  A[CI Gate Trigger] --> B{Policy Check}
  B --> C[Sidecar Injected]
  B --> D[AuthorizationPolicy Applied]
  D --> E[Deployment Validated]

## Why This Is Asked
Tests understanding of Kubernetes network security basics and CI gates using simple Bash checks. It also tests encoding a defensive posture with per-Namespace policies and simple allowlists. The candidate demonstrates crafting minimal, maintainable checks and integrating them into CI.

- Kubernetes NetworkPolicy basics
- Namespace-scoped policy enforcement
- GitHub Actions simple gate

## Code Example
```bash
# check-netpol.sh (simplified)
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
DIR=${1:-manifests}
for ns in $(yq e '.kind=="Namespace"|.metadata.name' -N "$DIR" | sort -u); do
  if ! grep -R --quiet "NetworkPolicy" "$DIR/$ns" 2>/dev/null; then
    echo "Missing NetworkPolicy in namespace $ns"; exit 1
  fi
done
```

questions.explanation:
This question tests the ability to design policy-driven image provenance using OPA Gatekeeper across standard Kubernetes resources and custom resources, including CI validation.

Key Concepts

- Gatekeeper, ConstraintTemplate, and Constraint resources
- Cosign signatures and Rekor transparency log verification
- CustomResourceDefinitions (CRDs) and cross-resource policy enforcement

Code Example

```yaml
# ConstraintTemplate: image provenance
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: imageprovenance
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: ImageProvenance
      validation:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          properties:
            rekorPublicKey:
              type: string
  targets:
  - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
    rego: |
      package imageprovenance
      
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        input.review.kind.kind == "Deployment"
        image := input.review.object.spec.template.spec.containers[_].image
        not verify_cosign(image, input.parameters.rekorPublicKey)
        msg := sprintf("Image %s failed cosign verification", [image])
      }
      
      verify_cosign(image, key) {
        # cosign verify --key=<key> <image> via webhook/agent
        # Returns true if signature valid, false otherwise
      }
```

```yaml
# Constraint
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: ImageProvenance
metadata:
  name: require-signed-images
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
    - apiGroups: ["apps"]
      kinds: ["Deployment"]
    - apiGroups: ["example.com"]
      kinds: ["AppConfig"]
  parameters:
    rekorPublicKey: |
      -----BEGIN PUBLIC KEY-----
      ...
      -----END PUBLIC KEY-----
```

```yaml
# Failing manifest (unsigned image)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: unsigned-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: unsigned

      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx:1.21
```

```yaml

apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: signed-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: signed
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: ghcr.io/owner/image:v1.0.0
```

CI Verification Steps

```yaml
verify-images:
  script:
    - cosign verify --key=cosign.pub $IMAGE
    - kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f deployment.yaml
```

questions.question: In a mono-repo with Kubernetes manifests for multiple services, design a beginner-friendly CI gate that enforces that every resource with metadata.namespace uses a non-default namespace from an allowed set (prod, staging, dev) and that no resource uses default or is missing a namespace. Provide a minimal GitHub Actions workflow snippet, a failing manifest using default, a passing manifest using prod, and how to verify locally and in CI?
questions.answer:
Enforce that every Kubernetes manifest with metadata.namespace uses an allowed namespace (prod, staging, dev) and never uses default or leaves namespace unspecified. Implement a GitHub Actions CI gate that runs a shell script using yq to validate all manifests in the repository.

**GitHub Actions workflow:**

```yaml
name: Validate Namespaces
on: [pull_request]

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/setup-yq@v1
      - name: Check namespaces
        run: |
          for f in $(find . -name '*.yaml' -o -'); do
           name '*.yml kind=$(yq -r '.kind // ""' "$f")
            ns=$(yq -r '.metadata.namespace // ""' "$f")
            
            if [ -z "$kind" ]; then
              continue
            fi
            
            if [[ "$kind" =~ ^(ClusterRole|ClusterRoleBinding|CustomResourceDefinition|Nodes|APIService)$ ]]; then
              continue
            fi
            
            if [ -z "$ns" ]; then
              echo "ERROR: Missing namespace in $f"
              exit 1
            fi
            
            if [[ ! "$ns" =~ ^(prod|staging|dev)$ ]]; then
              echo "ERROR: Invalid namespace '$ns' in $f (must be prod, staging, or dev)"
              exit 1
            fi
          done
```

**Failing manifest (uses default):**
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: my-config
  namespace: default
data:
  key: value
```

**Passing manifest (uses prod):**
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: my-config
  namespace: prod
data:
  key: value
```

**Local verification:**
```bash
# Install yq
brew install yq

# Run the validation script
for f in $(find . -name '*.yaml' -o -name '*.yml'); do
  kind=$(yq -r '.kind // ""' "$f")
  ns=$(yq -r '.metadata.namespace // ""' "$f")
  
  if [ -z "$kind" ]; then
    continue
  fi
  
  if [[ "$kind" =~ ^(ClusterRole|ClusterRoleBinding|CustomResourceDefinition|Nodes|APIService)$ ]]; then
    continue
  fi
  
  if [ -z "$ns" ]; then
    echo "ERROR: Missing namespace in $f"
    exit 1
  fi
  
  if [[ ! "$ns" =~ ^(prod|staging|dev)$ ]]; then
    echo "ERROR: Invalid namespace '$ns' in $f (must be prod, staging, or dev)"
    exit 1
  fi
done
```

**CI verification:**
The GitHub Actions workflow runs automatically on every pull request. If any namespace-scoped manifest violates the policy, the workflow fails and blocks the PR merge.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question tests understanding of basic CI gates for Kubernetes manifests and how to enforce namespace discipline across a multi-service repository. It demonstrates practical knowledge of YAML parsing, GitHub Actions, and Kubernetes resource management.

## Key Concepts

- **YAML manifest parsing with yq**: Using yq to extract and validate metadata.namespace fields across all Kubernetes manifests
- **Namespace discipline**: Enforcing non-default namespaces prevents accidental deployments to unintended environments and ensures proper resource isolation
- **Lightweight CI gates**: Simple, fast validation scripts every PR without that run on requiring external tools or complex infrastructure
- **Cluster-scoped vs namespace-scoped resources**: Understanding when to omit namespace (ClusterRole, CRDs) versus when it must be present (Deployment, Service, ConfigMap)

## What Separates Good Answers from Great Ones

A great answer recognizes that not all Kubernetes resources are namespace-scoped. Cluster-scoped resources like ClusterRole, ClusterRoleBinding, CustomResourceDefinition, and Nodes must be excluded from this validation. The candidate should handle this edge case gracefully, either by skipping these resource types or by documenting the assumption that only namespace-scoped resources are being validated.

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical hardening policies across CI and runtime, not just definitions.

## Key Concepts
- Kyverno policy drafting, matches, and deny rules
- Pod security basics: readOnlyRootFilesystem, allowPrivilegeEscalation, capabilities
- CI/CD integration and runtime admission flow; namespace-based exceptions

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-container-hardening
spec:
  validationFailureAction: fail
  rules:
  - name: deny-privileged-containers
    match:
      resources:
        kinds: ["Pod"]
    deny:
    - conditions:
      - key: '{{request.object.spec.containers[*].securityContext.privileged}}'
        operator: Equals
        value: 'true'
  - name: enforce-secure-rootfs
    match:
      resources:
        kinds: ["Pod"]
    deny:
    - conditions:
      - key: '{{request.object.spec.containers[*].securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem}}'
        operator: NotIn
        value: ['true']
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test namespace-level exemptions?
- How would you surface policy violations in CI dashboards and alerts?

## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to combine supply-chain security (cosign, SBOM) with policy-as-code (OPA Gatekeeper) in a multi-tenant, regulated context. Demonstrates end-to-end thinking from CI to runtime admission.

```yaml
spec:
```

## Why This Is Asked
This question probes practical policy design at scale, focusing on admission controls, RBAC hygiene, and network isolation in a multi-cloud Kubernetes environment. It tests how a candidate translates security requirements into Gatekeeper constraints and integrates them into CI.

## Key Concepts
- Open Policy Agent (OPA) Gatekeeper constraint templates
- Kubernetes security: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, privilege constraints
- RBAC hardening: disallow cluster-admin bindings, namespace-label exceptions
- NetworkPolicy defaults and whitelisting
- CI integration: manifest validation, test manifests, failing/passing samples

## Code Example
```yaml
# ConstraintTemplate: noPrivileged
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8snopriv
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sNoPriv
  targets:
  - target: AdmissionReview
    rego: |
      package k8snopriv
      violation["privileged container"] {
        input.review.object.spec.containers[_].securityContext.privileged == true
      }
```

```yaml
# Constraint
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sNoPriv
metadata:
  name: no-privileged-containers
spec:
  enforcementAction: deny
```

- How would you manage exceptions for ops-managed namespaces without weakening security?

## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to implement SBOM-based attestations and runtime provenance checks, plus how to wire CI gates with Kosign and CycloneDX.

## Key Concepts

- Image provenance, cosign signatures, attestations
- CycloneDX SBOMs, embedded in image annotations
- GitHub Actions gating, runtime enforcement (Kyverno/OPA)

## Code Example

```yaml
name: prod-provenance
on:
  pull_request:
    paths:
      - manifests/prod/**
jobs:
  gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: setup cosign
        run: |
          curl -sSfL https://github.com/sigstore/cosign/releases/download/v1.10.0/cosign-linux-amd64 -o cosign
          chmod +x cosign
          sudo mv cosign /usr/local/bin/
      - name: verify images
        run: |
          cosign verify -key cosign.pub registry.example.com/namespace/service:tag
      - name: verify SBOM attestation
        run: |
          cyclone_dx_attest --image registry.example.com/namespace/service:tag --attest
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you handle attestation revocation and key rotation?
- How to scale SBOM checks across many services and environments?

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Demonstrates practicalPolicy as code design with Gatekeeper/OPA, multi-cloud awareness, and enforcing both pod and network baselines.

## Key Concepts
- Gatekeeper/OPA policy authoring and constraint templates
- Pod Security Standards implications (non-root, readOnlyRootFilesystem, privileged escalation)
- Per-namespace NetworkPolicy enforcement and centralized egress allowlist
- Local validation and CI integration (dry-run, audits)

## Code Example
```yaml
# ConstraintTemplate (K8sSecurityBaseline)
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8securitybaseline
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sSecurityBaseline
  targets:
  - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
    rego: |
      package k8securitybaseline
      violation["RunAsNonRoot"] { input.review.object.spec.containers[_].securityContext.runAsNonRoot != true }
      violation["ReadOnlyRoot"] { input.review.object.spec.containers[_].securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem != true }
      violation["NoNETAdmin"] { input.review.object.spec.containers[_].securityContext.capabilities.NET_ADMIN == true }
```
```yaml
# Constraint (example)
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sSecurityBaseline
metadata:
  name: require-baseline
spec:
  match:
   Kinds:
    - apiGroups: ["apps"]
      kinds: ["Deployment"]
```

- How would you test policy drift and maintain allowlists across clouds?

questions.question: In a Kubernetes GitOps repo used by a fintech platform, design a policy gate that enforces runtime security by requiring every Deployment to set securityContext (runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, and drop all capabilities) and to specify per-container resources. questions.explanation:
Why This Is Asked
- Tests policy-as-code skills for multi-tenant Kubernetes environments
- Evaluates ability to translate security requirements into enforceable runtime and CI gates

Key Concepts
- Kyverno, Pod Security Standards, Resource limits, CI gates, Drift detection

Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-secure-deployments
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  background: true
  rules:
  - name: security-context
    match:
      resources:
        kinds: ["Deployment"]
    validate:
      message: "Each container must define a secure securityContext with runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, and drop all capabilities"
      pattern:
        spec:
          template:
            spec:
              containers:
              - securityContext:
                  runAsNonRoot: "true"
                  readOnlyRootFilesystem: "true"
                  capabilities:
                    drop: ["ALL"]
  - name: require-resources
    match:
      resources:
        kinds: ["Deployment"]
    validate:
      message: "Each container must specify resource limits and requests"
      pattern:
        spec:
          template:
            spec:
              containers:
              - resources:
                  limits:
                    memory: "?*"
                    cpu: "?*"
                  requests:
                    memory: "?*"
                    cpu: "?*"
```

Failing Test Manifest
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: insecure-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: insecure
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx:latest
```

Passing Test Manifest
```yaml
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: secure-app
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: secure
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx:latest
        securityContext:
          runAsNonRoot: true
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
          capabilities:
            drop: ["ALL"]
        resources:
          requests:
            memory: "64Mi"
            cpu: "250m"
          limits:
            memory: "128Mi"
            cpu: "500m"
```

CI Validation Steps
```bash
# Install Kyverno CLI
brew install kyverno

# Validate policies against manifests
kyverno apply policy.yaml --manifest deployment.yaml

# CI/CD integration example (GitHub Actions)
- name: Validate Kubernetes manifests
  run: |
    kyverno apply policy.yaml -f deploy/
```

Runtime Gate Demonstration
```bash
# Apply the policy cluster-wide
kubectl apply -f policy.yaml

# Verify policy is active
kubectl get clusterpolicy require-secure-deployments

# Attempt to deploy non-compliant manifest (will fail)
kubectl apply -f insecure-deployment.yaml
# Error: Validation failed: Each container must define a secure securityContext...

# Deploy compliant manifest (will succeed)
kubectl apply -f secure-deployment.yaml
# deployment.apps/secure-app created
```

questions.question: Design an admission gate for a multi-tenant Kubernetes cluster used across Bloomberg, Scale AI, and Google that enforces runtime security baselines via policy-as-code. Require non-root containers, readOnlyRootFilesystem, no privileged, no hostNetwork/hostPID, and no hostPath volumes unless explicitly allowed. Provide an Open Policy Agent (rego) policy, a failing manifest, a passing manifest, and CI verification steps?
questions.answer: Implement an OPA gate with a Rego policy that rejects Pod specs when any container runs as root, uses privileged mode, does not have readOnlyRootFilesystem set to true, enables hostNetwork or hostPID, or uses hostPath volumes (unless explicitly allowed). Include the Rego policy, a failing manifest, a passing manifest, and CI verification steps.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This question evaluates the ability to encode security baselines as policy-as-code and enforce them in CI/CD pipelines for multi-tenant clusters. It tests understanding of threat modeling, policy granularity, and how to allow exceptions in a controlled manner.

## Key Concepts

- **Open Policy Agent (OPA) with Rego**: Declarative policy language for Kubernetes admission control
- **Pod Security Standards**: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem, privilege escalation, and security profiles
- **Volume Security**: hostPath, hostNetwork, and hostPID considerations in multi-tenant environments

## Rego Policy

```rego
package kubernetes.admission

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "Pod"
  container := input.request.object.spec.containers[_]
  container.securityContext.runAsNonRoot != true
  msg := "Containers must run as non-root (runAsNonRoot: true)"
}

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "Pod"
  container := input.request.object.spec.containers[_]
  container.securityContext.privileged == true
  msg := "Privileged containers are not allowed"
}

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "Pod"
  container := input.request.object.spec.containers[_]
  container.securityContext.readOnlyRootFilesystem != true
  msg := "Containers must use readOnlyRootFilesystem"
}

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "Pod"
  input.request.object.spec.hostNetwork == true
  msg := "hostNetwork is not allowed"
}

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "Pod"
  input.request.object.spec.hostPID == true
  msg := "hostPID is not allowed"
}

deny[msg] {
  input.request.kind.kind == "Pod"
  volume := input.request.object.spec.volumes[_]
  volume.hostPath
  not allowed_hostpath[volume.hostPath.path]
  msg := sprintf("hostPath volume %s is not allowed", [volume.hostPath.path])
}

allowed_hostpath {
  input.request.namespace == "kube-system"
}

allowed_hostpath[path] {
  allowed_paths := [""]
  path := allowed_paths[_]
}
```

## Failing Manifest

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: insecure-pod
  namespace: default
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:latest
    securityContext:
      runAsNonRoot: false
      privileged: true
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: false
  hostNetwork: true
  hostPID: true
  volumes:
  - name: data
    hostPath:
      path: /var/data
```

## Passing Manifest

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
  namespace: production
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:stable
    securityContext:
      runAsNonRoot: true
      privileged: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      seccompProfile:
        type: RuntimeDefault
  securityContext:
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  volumes:
  - name: cache
    emptyDir: {}
```

## CI Verification Steps

1. **Install OPA CLI**: `brew install opa` or download from https://www.openpolicyagent.org/docs/
2. **Test Rego policy**: `opa eval --format pretty --data policy.rego --input failing-pod.json "data.kubernetes.admission.deny"`
3. **Verify failing manifest**: Should return denial messages for each violated policy
4. **Verify passing manifest**: Should return empty deny array
5. **CI/CD Integration**: Add OPA gate to your pipeline:
   ```yaml
   # GitHub Actions example
   - name: Run OPA Gate
     run: |
       opa eval --format json --data policy.rego --input pod.json "data.kubernetes.admission.deny" > results.json
       if [ $(jq '.result[0].expressions[0].value | length' results.json) -gt 0 ]; then
         echo "Policy violations found:"
         jq '.result[0].expressions[0].value' results.json
         exit 1
       fi
   ```
6. **Deploy to cluster**: Apply the OPA Gatekeeper constraints via Gatekeeper's ConstraintTemplate system.

  B --> C{Policy Pass?}

  C -->|No| E[Block & Report]

questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design multi-source telemetry-driven detection for data exfiltration in complex cloud-native data platforms, balancing legitimate data workflows with security controls. Evaluates incident response readiness and depth of tooling integration.

## Key Concepts

- Unity Catalog audit logs and Delta Lake metadata
- IAM/STS activity and rule-based access governance
- External data egress controls and approval workflows
- VPC flow logs, Kubernetes data plane telemetry, and export APIs
- Baseline-based anomaly detection and auto-containment

## Code Example

```python
def is_suspicious(event, whitelist, THRESHOLD_GB):
    if event.type != "EXPORT":
        return False
    if event.destination_bucket not in whitelist:
        return True
    if event.size_gb > THRESHOLD_GB:
        return True
    return False
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you minimize false positives while maintaining network security?
- What metrics would you monitor to tune thresholds over time?

questions.question: In a Kubernetes manifests repo for a multi-service app, design a beginner-friendly CI gate that requires every Deployment to include a PodDisruptionBudget (PDB) with minAvailable: 1 and ensures all containers set readOnlyRootFilesystem: true. Provide a minimal GitHub Actions workflow, a failing manifest missing PDB and with readOnlyRootFilesystem: false, a passing manifest with PDB and readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, and describe how to verify locally and in CI?
questions.answer: Provide a GitHub Actions gate that validates every Deployment has a PodDisruptionBudget (minAvailable: 1) and that all containers use readOnlyRootFilesystem: true. Deliver a failing manifest missing P
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests a candidate's ability to enforce governance rules on Kubernetes manifests via CI, start with beginner checks, and wire local + CI verification.

## Key Concepts

- PodDisruptionBudget basics
- Pod security via readOnlyRootFilesystem
- GitHub Actions workflow basics
- local kubectl dry-run validation

## Code Example

```yaml
# failing manifest (no PDB, readOnlyRootFilesystem: false)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: svc-a
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: my-reg/app-a:latest
        securityContext:
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: false
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
```

```yaml
# passing manifest (PDB + readOnlyRootFilesystem: true)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: svc-a
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: my-reg/app-a@sha256:abc
        securityContext:
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
---
apiVersion: policy/v1
kind: PodDisruptionBudget
metadata:
  name: pdb-svc-a
spec:
  minAvailable: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: svc-a
```

```yaml
# GitHub Actions gate (minimal)
name: PDB + ReadOnlyRootFilesystem gate
on:
  pull_request:
    types: [opened, synchronize]
jobs:
  ck:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - uses: azure/setup-kubectl@v3
        with:
          version: 'latest'
      - name: Lint manifests
        run: |
          kubectl version --client
          echo 'Lint step placeholder for yamllint/jsonlint'
      - name: Validate PDB and readOnlyRootFilesystem
        run: |
          # simple validation example
          if ! kubectl get pdb pdb-svc-a >/dev/null 2>&1; then
            echo 'PDB missing for svc-a'; exit 1
          fi
          if ! grep -R "readOnlyRootFilesystem: true" -n k8s || true; then
            echo 'Some container not enforcing readOnlyRootFilesystem'; exit 1
          fi
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you scale this gate to cover all namespaces and workloads beyond Deployments? 
- What are the trade-offs of enforcing readOnlyRootFilesystem strictly vs allowing certain privileged ops for debugging?

questions.question: In a Kubernetes manifests repo powering a multi-service app, design a beginner-friendly CI gate that rejects deprecated API versions and enforces that every resource declares an app and version label, while ensuring container images come from an internal registry with a digest (no tags). Provide a minimal GitHub Actions workflow, a failing manifest (using apps/v1beta1 Deployment and missing labels), and a passing manifest (apps/v1 Deployment with labels and image digest). How would you verify locally and in CI?
questions.answer: Describe a script-driven gate: fail if any resource uses a deprecated API version, lacks labels app/version, or has image with a tag or external registry. Include a minimal GitHub Actions workflow and
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests basic manifest validation, API version awareness, and simple, repeatable CI gating for multi-service apps.

## Key Concepts
- Kubernetes manifests, apiVersion, kind
- Labels discipline (app, version)
- Image digests and internal registries
- Lightweight CI gates (GitHub Actions) and script-based checks

## Code Example
```bash
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail
for f in $(git ls-files '*.yaml' '*.yml'); do
  api=$(yq e '.apiVersion' "$f")
  if [[ "$api" == "apps/v1beta1" || "$api" == "extensions/v1beta1" ]]; then
    echo "Deprecated apiVersion: $api in $f"; exit 1
  fi
  if ! yq e 'has(.metadata.labels.app) and has(.metadata.labels.version)' "$f" >/dev/null; then
    echo "Missing labels in $f"; exit 1
  fi
  if ! yq e '.spec.template.spec.containers[].image' "$f" | grep -q '@sha256'; then
    echo "Image must use digest in $f"; exit 1
  fi
done
```

```yaml
# Failing manifest (deprecated API, missing labels)
apiVersion: apps/v1beta1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: example
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: internal-registry.example.com/app/web:latest
```

```yaml
# Passing manifest (apps/v1, labels, image digest)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: example
  labels:
    app: web
    version: v1
spec:
  replicas: 2
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: web
        version: v1
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: web
        image: internal-registry.example.com/app/web@sha256:deadbeef...
```

```yaml
# Minimal GitHub Actions workflow
name: Validate manifests
on:
  push: { branches: [ main ] }
jobs:
  gate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Validate manifests
        run: |
          bash ./ci/validate-manifests.sh
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt this gate for a multi-tenant cluster with per-namespace policies?
- How would you report detailed failure metrics to the team when gates fail?

questions.question: In a data ingestion pipeline for a daily analytics dataset, design a beginner-friendly approach to prevent schema drift when loading daily CSVs into a Snowflake staging table before merging into production. Provide a minimal Python snippet that reads a CSV and validates: required columns exist (id:int, date:date, amount:float, category:string), no extra columns, and types match; fail on mismatch and missing/extra columns. Outline a GitHub Actions step to run the script and block the load if it fails. Show a failing and a passing CSV header example?
questions.answer: Use a small Python validator validate_csv.py that reads the header, ensures exactly the 4 fields, and converts id/date/amount to int, date, float. If a mismatch or conversion error occurs, exit(1). In
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests practical data validation in CI/CD with a concrete schema and gating.

## Key Concepts
- CSV header validation
- Type coercion and data quality
- CI gate integration (GitHub Actions)

## Code Example
```python
import csv, sys
from datetime import datetime

EXPECTED = {
  'id': int,
  'date': 'date',
  'amount': float,
  'category': str
}

DATE_FMT = '%Y-%m-%d'

def parse_row(row):
  row['id'] = int(row['id'])
  row['date'] = datetime.strptime(row['date'], DATE_FMT).date()
  row['amount'] = float(row['amount'])
  row['category'] = str(row['category'])
  return row

def main(path):
  with open(path, newline='') as f:
    reader = csv.DictReader(f)
    header = set(reader.fieldnames or [])
    if header != set(EXPECTED.keys()):
      print('Header mismatch:', reader.fieldnames)
      sys.exit(1)
    for r in reader:
      try:
        parse_row(r)
      except Exception as e:
        print('Type/Parse error', e, r)
        sys.exit(1)
  print('OK')

if __name__ == '__main__':
  sys.exit(main(sys.argv[1]))
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you adapt for evolving schemas without breaking the pipeline?
- How would you test locally with representative failing/passing CSVs?

questions.question: Design a Gatekeeper/OPA policy for a multi-cloud Kubernetes platform that enforces runtime network and identity controls: every Deployment namespace must have a NetworkPolicy denying ingress by default and only allowing whitelisted sources; pods must have automountServiceAccountToken disabled unless the service account is explicitly trusted; and all outbound TLS traffic must be mutual TLS validated by a central CA. Provide a minimal ConstraintTemplate, a failing manifest, a passing manifest, and CI verification steps?
questions.answer: Use Gatekeeper with a ConstraintTemplate enforcing: (1) Namespace has a deny-all ingress NetworkPolicy with explicit allowlist; (2) Pod automountServiceAccountToken: false unless SA annotated trusted=
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to express policy-as-code for security/compliance in multi-cloud clusters, combining network isolation, identity controls, and outbound security.

## Key Concepts
- Gatekeeper/OPA ConstraintTemplate
- NetworkPolicy as runtime guard
- Pod securityContext and automountServiceAccountToken control
- mTLS and central CA validation for outbound traffic
- CI verification using kubectl, gatekeeper audits

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8snetworkidentity
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sNetworkIdentityConstraint
  targets:
  - target: Kubernetes
    rego: |
      package k8snetworkidentity
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        input.review.kind == "Deployment"
        not has_ns_deny_ingress(input)
        msg := "Deployment namespace must have a deny-ingress NetworkPolicy and allow only whitelisted sources."
      }
```

```yaml
# Failing manifest (no deny ingress and automount enabled)
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: example-fail
  namespace: dev
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: example-fail
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: example-fail
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx:alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
```

```yaml
# Passing manifest (deny ingress, trusted SA, automount disabled)
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: app-sa
  namespace: dev
  annotations:
    trusted.example.io: "true"
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: example-pass
  namespace: dev
spec:
  replicas: 1
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: example-pass
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: example-pass
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: app-sa
      automountServiceAccountToken: false
      containers:
      - name: app
        image: nginx:alpine
        ports:
        - containerPort: 80
```

```yaml
# Minimal NetworkPolicy (deny ingress by default, allow from trusted namespace)
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-ingress
  namespace: dev
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - namespaceSelector:
        matchLabels:
          env: trusted
```

## CI Verification Steps
- Gatekeeper audit run in CI to ensure constraints are evaluated.
- kubectl apply --dry-run=client -f failing.yaml should trigger policy violations.
- kubectl apply -f passing.yaml should succeed and Gatekeeper should record no violations.
- Validate that namespaces have the required NetworkPolicy objects and that deployments use trusted SAs with automountServiceAccountToken disabled.
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  A[Policy Design] --> B[NetworkPolicy Requirement]
  B --> C[Identity Control]
  C --> D[Outbound TLS/mTLS]
  D --> E[CI Verification]

## Why This Is Asked
This question evaluates practical policy design for multi-tenant data platforms, including CRD usage, Vault integration, and lineage emission, plus CI coverage and performance considerations.

```rego
  input.review.request.kind == "AdmissionReview"

  env := cont.env[_]
  env.value != null
```

questions.answer: Design a CI gate validating Istio CRs for mTLS and egress. It rejects mutations that omit peerAuthentication mtls: STRICT and that add EnvoyFilter/modify Sidecar to bypass mtls. Use a policy (OPA) plu
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
The question tests enforcement of mutual TLS and network egress in a multi-tenant, large-scale cluster, with production-grade tooling.

## Key Concepts
- Istio PeerAuthentication mtls: STRICT
- EnvoyFilter and Sidecar config drift detection
- Namespace egress controls and policy-as-code
- GitHub Actions, kubectl diff, and OPA gating

## Code Example
```yaml
# Failing manifest: mtls disabled
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: app
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: DISABLE
```
```yaml
# Passing manifest: mtls STRICT and egress restricted
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: default
  namespace: app
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: STRICT
```

```yaml
# EnvoyFilter that would bypass mtls (flagged by gate)
apiVersion: networking.istio.io/v1alpha3
kind: EnvoyFilter
metadata:
  name: bypass-mtls
  namespace: app
spec:
  workloadSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: sample
  configPatches:
  - applyTo: NETWORK_FILTER
    patch:
      operation: MERGE
      value:
        name: some_filter
        config:
          mtls_bypass: true
```

## Follow-up Questions

```rego
package k8ssecurity
```

```yaml
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
```

questions.question: In a Kubernetes manifests repo for a multi-team app, design a beginner-friendly CI gate (GitHub Actions + Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper) that rejects manifests using hostNetwork/hostPort, hostPath volumes, or privileged containers, and enforces that images originate from approved registries with a matching imagePullPolicy; provide a minimal policy, a failing manifest (hostPath and hostNetwork), a passing manifest, and describe local and CI validation?
questions.answer: Design a CI gate using Kyverno to enforce Kubernetes security policies at the cluster level. Create a ClusterPolicy named `deny-host-access` with rules to block hostNetwork, hostPort, hostPath, and privileged containers. Add a second ClusterPolicy for AllowedRegistries to enforce approved registries and require matching imagePullPolicy. In CI, spin up a KinD cluster, install Kyverno, apply the policies, and validate manifests before merging pull requests.
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Security hardening of Kubernetes manifests is a practical, repeatable gate in real projects. This tests understanding of policy-as-code, tooling choices (Kyverno vs Gatekeeper), constraint design for host access controls and image source controls, and CI integration in GitHub Actions with ephemeral test clusters.

## Key Concepts
- Policy-as-code with Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper
- Constraints for host access (hostNetwork, hostPath, hostPort, privileged containers)
- Image source controls (allowed registries) and imagePullPolicy enforcement
- CI integration in GitHub Actions using KinD or similar ephemeral clusters

## Code Example
```yaml
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: deny-host-access
spec:
  rules:
  - name: block-host-network
    match:
      resources:
        kinds:
        - Pod
    validate:
      message: "hostNetwork is not allowed"
      pattern:
        spec:
          hostNetwork: "false"
  - name: block-host-path
    match:
      resources:
        kinds:
        - Pod
    validate:
      message: "hostPath volumes are not allowed"
      deny:
        conditions:
        - key: "{{ contains(element.volumes[].hostPath, '') }}"
          operator: "NotEqual"
          value: ""
```

## Minimal Policy (Combined)
```yaml
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: security-guardrails
spec:
  rules:
  - name: deny-host-network-port
    match:
      resources:
        kinds:
        - Pod
    validate:
      message: "hostNetwork and hostPort are not permitted"
      pattern:
        spec:
          hostNetwork: "false"
          hostPort: "0"
  - name: deny-host-path
    match:
      resources:
        kinds:
        - Pod
    validate:
      message: "hostPath volumes are not permitted"
      deny:
        conditions:
        - key: "{{ @ }}"
          operator: "AnyNotIn"
          value: [""]
      foreach:
        - list: "spec.volumes[?hostPath]"
          deny: true
  - name: deny-privileged-containers
    match:
      resources:
        kinds:
        - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Privileged containers are not permitted"
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - securityContext:
              privileged: "false"
  - name: allowed-registries
    match:
      resources:
        kinds:
        - Pod
    validate:
      message: "Images must come from approved registries"
      pattern:
        spec:
          containers:
          - image: "registry.company.com/*"
            imagePullPolicy: "Always"
```

## Failing Manifest (hostPath + hostNetwork)
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: insecure-pod
spec:
  hostNetwork: true
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: docker.io/myapp:latest
    securityContext:
      privileged: true
    volumeMounts:
    - name: host-volume
      mountPath: /data
  volumes:
  - name: host-volume
    hostPath:
      path: /etc
```

## Passing Manifest
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: registry.company.com/myapp:v1.0
    imagePullPolicy: Always
```

## Local Validation
```bash
# Install Kyverno locally
brew install kyverno

# Apply policies
kubectl apply -f security-guardrails.yaml

# Validate a manifest
kyverno validate insecure-pod.yaml
```

## CI Validation (GitHub Actions)
```yaml
name: Validate Manifests
on: [pull_request]
jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
    - uses: actions/checkout@v4
    - name: Create KinD cluster
      run: kind create cluster
    - name: Install Kyverno
      run: |
        kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno/releases/latest/download/install.yaml
        kubectl wait --for=condition=ready pod -l app=kyverno -n kyverno --timeout=120s
    - name: Apply policies
      run: kubectl apply -f security-guardrails.yaml
    - name: Validate manifests
      run: |
        kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f insecure-pod.yaml || echo "Expected failure"
        kubectl apply --dry-run=server -f secure-pod.yaml
```

questions.question: In a repo containing Terraform modules and Kubernetes manifests for a small app, design a beginner-friendly CI gate that prevents storing secrets in plaintext inside any .tfvars or values.yaml files and ensures secrets come from a centralized vault or Secrets Manager reference. Provide a minimal GitHub Actions workflow, a failing snippet with a hard-coded secret, a passing snippet using a secrets reference, and explain how to validate locally and in CI?
questions.answer: Implement a GitHub Actions gate that scans .tfvars and values.yaml for plaintext secrets and rejects pushes containing them. Use grep -Ei with patterns like aws_access_key_id|aws_secret_access_key|pas
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

This checks basic IaC hygiene: no secrets in code, and a simple path to secrets management. It’s a realistic beginner task that validates tooling fluency without requiring advanced security parity.

## Key Concepts

- Secrets in IaC and version control
- Lightweight CI gates and deterministic feedback
- Secrets management references (Secrets Manager/Vault) in IaC

## Code Example

```bash
name: Secrets Scan
on: [push, pull_request]
jobs:
  scan:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      - name: Scan for secrets
        run: |
          set -euo pipefail
          if grep -RInE --include='*.tfvars' --include='*.yaml' -e 'aws_access_key_id|aws_secret_access_key|password|token' .; then
            echo "Secret found"; exit 1
          fi
```

## Follow-up Questions

- How would you adapt this gate for more secret patterns across multiple repos?
- What are the trade-offs between blacklists vs. allowlists for secret patterns?

questions.question: In a multi-tenant Kubernetes platform, design a policy-as-code gate that enforces container security posture: every Pod must run as non-root, have readOnlyRootFilesystem, and drop all capabilities. Use Kyverno to enforce on Deployments/StatefulSets. Provide a minimal ClusterPolicy, a failing manifest (root, privileged), a passing manifest (non-root, ROFS, cap drop), and CI verification steps?
questions.answer: Propose a Kyverno-based gate enforcing: runAsNonRoot: true, readOnlyRootFilesystem: true, and capabilities.drop: [ALL] for all containers. Include a minimal ClusterPolicy, a failing manifest (root use
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Tests ability to translate security policies into policy-as-code, integrate with CI, and reason about multi-tenant risk.

## Key Concepts
- Kyverno cluster policies
- Pod security posture: runAsNonRoot, readOnlyRootFilesystem
- Capabilities and privilege minimization
- CI integration with cluster validation

## Code Example
```javascript
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-container-security
spec:
  rules:
  - name: enforce-security-context
    match:
      resources:
        kinds: ["Deployment","StatefulSet","DaemonSet","Pod"]
    validate:
      message: "Containers must set non-root, ROFS and drop caps"
      pattern:
        spec:
          template:
            spec:
              containers:
              - name: "*"
                securityContext:
                  runAsNonRoot: true
                  readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
                  capabilities:
                    drop: ["ALL"]
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test this at scale across namespaces?
- What are common edge cases (InitContainers, sidecars, imagePullPolicy)?

questions.question: In a Kubernetes-based data-processing platform deployed across multi-cloud (e.g., AWS/GCP) used by major customers, implement an Open Policy Agent Gatekeeper policy to enforce image provenance and pod hardening at admission. The policy must (a) allow only images from registry.example.com with a non-latest tag and a digest, (b) require resources.requests and resources.limits on all containers, (c) require securityContext.runAsNonRoot and readOnlyRootFilesystem and disallow privilegeEscalation, (d) require Namespace to have data-platform=true, and (e) return clear denial messages. Provide the ConstraintTemplate, Constraint, minimal failing/passing manifests, and CI validation steps?
questions.answer: Implement Gatekeeper policy: a ConstraintTemplate ImageProvenanceAndSecurity with constraints enforcing image registry registry.example.com, tag not latest, and image.digest present; a PodHardening co
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
Real-world multi-tenant data platforms require strong admission control to enforce image provenance and host security postures across clouds. Gatekeeper with Rego enables centralized policy with testable manifests.

## Key Concepts
- Open Policy Agent Gatekeeper
- ConstraintTemplate and Constraint objects
- Image provenance: registry, non-latest, digest
- Pod hardening: resources, securityContext, privilegeEscalation
- Namespace labeling and admission scope
- CI validation: conftest, gatekeeper audit, sample manifests

## Code Example
```yaml
# ConstraintTemplate (ImageProvenanceAndSecurity)
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: imageprovenance
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: ImageProvenanceConstraint
  targets:
    - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
      rego: |
        package imageprovenance
        violation[{"msg": msg}] {
          some i
          container := input.review.object.spec.template.spec.containers[i]
          image := container.image
          startswith(image, "registry.example.com/")
          not endswith(image, ":latest")
          some digest := input.review.object.spec.template.spec.containers[i].imageDigest
          msg := "image must be from registry.example.com with digest"
        }
```
```yaml
# Constraint (ImageProvenanceConstraint)
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: ImageProvenanceConstraint
metadata:
  name: require-provenance
spec:
  enforcementAction: deny
```

```yaml
# Failing manifest: unsigned or latest, missing resources
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: data-processor
  namespace: data
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: proc
        image: registry.example.com/data/processor:latest
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
```

```yaml
# Passing manifest: compliant image, resources, and securityContext
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: data-processor
  namespace: data
spec:
  replicas: 1
  template:
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: proc
        image: registry.example.com/data/processor@sha256:abcdef...
        resources:
          requests:
            cpu: "100m"
            memory: "128Mi"
          limits:
            cpu: "500m"
            memory: "512Mi"
        securityContext:
          runAsNonRoot: true
          allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
          readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
```

## Follow-up Questions
- How would you test policy changes locally before applying to cluster?
- How would you roll out a breaking policy change across multiple namespaces without downtime?

questions.question: In a Kubernetes manifests mono-repo for a multi-service app, design a beginner-friendly CI gate (GitHub Actions + Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper) that requires every Namespace to have a default-deny NetworkPolicy and at least one Namespace-scoped policy restricting egress to approved external domains and internal services only. Provide a minimal policy, a failing manifest missing default-deny or egress rules, a passing manifest, and describe how to validate locally and in CI?
questions.answer:
Implement a CI gate using Kyverno or OPA Gatekeeper that enforces two requirements: (1) every Namespace must have a default-deny NetworkPolicy, and (2) every Namespace must have at least one egress rule allowing only approved external domains and internal cluster services.

**Minimal Policy (Kyverno example):**
```yaml
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-default-deny-networkpolicy
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  background: false
  rules:
    - name: check-default-deny-networkpolicy
      match:
        kinds:
          - kind: Namespace
      validate:
        message: "Every Namespace must have a default-deny NetworkPolicy"
        deny:
          conditions:
            - key: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
              operator: NotEquals
              value: ""
            - key: "{{ length(filter(requests('Networking.k8s.io/v1', 'NetworkPolicy', request.object.metadata.name), @.spec.policyTypes[*] | contains(@, 'Ingress'))) }}"
              operator: Equals
              value: "0"
            - key: "{{ length(filter(requests('Networking.k8s.io/v1', 'NetworkPolicy', request.object.metadata.name), @.spec.policyTypes[*] | contains(@, 'Egress'))) }}"
              operator: Equals
              value: "0"
---
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-egress-policy
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  background: false
  rules:
    - name: check-egress-rules
      match:
        kinds:
          - kind: Namespace
      validate:
        message: "Every Namespace must have egress rules allowing only approved domains and internal services"
        deny:
          conditions:
            - key: "{{request.object.metadata.name}}"
              operator: NotEquals
              value: ""
            - key: "{{ length(filter(requests('Networking.k8s.io/v1', 'NetworkPolicy', request.object.metadata.name), @.spec.egress)) }}"
              operator: Equals
              value: "0"
```

**Simplified Alternative Using Label Gating:**
```yaml
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-default-deny-networkpolicy
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-default-deny-label
      match:
        kinds:
          - kind: Namespace
      validate:
        message: "Every Namespace must have a default-deny NetworkPolicy"
        pattern:
          metadata:
            labels:
              security.kubernetes.io/default-deny: "true"
---
apiVersion: kyverno.io/v1
kind: ClusterPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-egress-policy
spec:
  validationFailureAction: enforce
  rules:
    - name: check-egress-rules
      match:
        kinds:
          - kind: NetworkPolicy
      validate:
        message: "Egress must restrict traffic to approved external domains and internal services only"
        deny:
          conditions:
            - key: "{{ spec.policyTypes }}"
              operator: AnyNotIn
              value: ["Ingress", "Egress"]
            - key: "{{ spec.egress[] }}"
              operator: AnyNotIn
              value:
                - to:
                    - namespaceSelector:
                        matchLabels:
                          kubernetes.io/metadata.name: internal-services
                  ports:
                    - protocol: TCP
                      port: 443
                - to:
                    - dnsSelector:
                        matchLabels:
                          domain: allowed.external.com
                  ports:
                    - protocol: TCP
                      port: 443
```

**Failing Manifest (missing default-deny and proper egress rules):**
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: payment-service
  labels:
    security.kubernetes.io/default-deny: "true"
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: payment-egress
  namespace: payment-service
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - podSelector: {}
```

**Passing Manifest:**
```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: Namespace
metadata:
  name: payment-service
  labels:
    security.kubernetes.io/default-deny: "true"
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny
  namespace: payment-service
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Ingress
    - Egress
---
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: payment-egress
  namespace: payment-service
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
    - Egress
  egress:
    - to:
        - namespaceSelector:
            matchLabels:
              kubernetes.io/metadata.name: internal-services
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 443
    - to:
        - dnsSelector:
            matchLabels:
              domain: allowed.external.com
      ports:
        - protocol: TCP
          port: 443
```

**Local Validation:**
```bash
# Using Kyverno CLI
kyverno apply policy.yaml --resource manifest.yaml --cluster

# Using OPA CLI (with Rego policies)
opa eval --input manifest.yaml --policy policy.rego --format pretty "data.policy.deny"

# Dry-run validation in Kubernetes
kubectl apply -f manifest.yaml --dry-run=server
```

**CI Validation (GitHub Actions):**
```yaml
name: Validate Kubernetes Manifests
on: [push, pull_request]

jobs:
  validate:
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      - uses: actions/checkout@v4
      
      - name: Set up Kyverno
        run: |
          curl -sLo kyverno.tar.gz https://github.com/kyverno/kyverno/releases/latest/download/kyverno-cli-linux-amd64.tar.gz
          tar -xzf kyverno.tar.gz
          sudo mv kyverno /usr/local/bin/
      
      - name: Validate namespace manifests
        run: |
          for manifest in manifests/**/*.yaml; do
            kyverno apply policy.yaml --resource "$manifest" --cluster || exit 1
          done
      
      - name: Validate NetworkPolicies
        run: |
          for ns in $(kubectl get ns -o jsonpath='{.items[*].metadata.name}'); do
            kubectl get networkpolicy -n "$ns" -o yaml > "/tmp/np_$ns.yaml"
            kyverno apply policy.yaml --resource "/tmp/np_$ns.yaml" --cluster || exit 1
          done
```

**OPA Gatekeeper Alternative:**
```yaml
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sRequiredNetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-default-deny
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        kinds: ["Namespace"]
  parameters:
    policyTypes: ["Ingress", "Egress"]
```
```rego
package k8srequirednetworkpolicy

deny[msg] {
  input.kind == "Namespace"
  not input.metadata.name
  msg := "Namespace must have a default-deny NetworkPolicy"
}
```
questions.explanation:
## Purpose

This question evaluates practical understanding of Kubernetes network security at the Namespace level and the ability to integrate policy tooling into CI pipelines. It demonstrates competence in specifying concrete, testable rules and validating them both locally and in CI.

## Key Concepts

- **Namespace-scoped NetworkPolicy** with a default-deny baseline that blocks all ingress and egress traffic by default
- **Egress restrictions** to approved external domains and internal services only
- **Kyverno** vs. **OPA Gatekeeper** for policy enforcement—Kyverno offers simpler YAML-based policies, while OPA Gatekeeper provides more expressive Rego-based logic
- **Local validation** using `kyverno apply` or `opa eval` commands
- **CI validation** within GitHub Actions workflows to catch policy violations before deployment

## Implementation Notes

1. **Default-deny NetworkPolicy** blocks all traffic by default; specific rules then allow permitted traffic
2. **Egress rules** should whitelist internal service namespaces and approved external domains
3. **Policy enforcement** can use `validationFailureAction: enforce` to block deployments or `audit` for monitoring
4. **CI gates** should validate manifests before they reach the cluster to catch issues early

questions.question: In a shared Kubernetes cluster servicing Snowflake and Nvidia workloads, implement an Open Policy Agent Gatekeeper policy to enforce container hardening. The gate should require RunAsNonRoot=true, ReadOnlyRootFilesystem=true, CapDrop: - ALL, and reject Privileged containers. Provide a minimal ConstraintTemplate and Constraint, plus failing and passing manifests, and describe CI and admission-time verification?
questions.answer: Implement Gatekeeper with ConstraintTemplate K8sContainerHardening enforcing RunAsNonRoot: true, ReadOnlyRootFilesystem: true, and CapDrop: ['ALL']; block Privileged: true. Include a failing manifest
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked
 Assesses ability to translate security policy into Gatekeeper rules for a multi-tenant cluster.

## Key Concepts
 - Open Policy Agent Gatekeeper
 - ConstraintTemplate, Constraint
 - Pod security hardening
 - Kubernetes admission webhook behavior

## Code Example
```yaml
# ConstraintTemplate example (high level)
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata: {name: K8sContainerHardening}
# ...
```
```yaml
# Constraint example
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sContainerHardeningConstraint
metadata: {name: harden-containers}
spec:
  match: { kinds: [{ apiGroups: [""], kinds: ["Pod"] }] }
  parameters:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
    dropCapabilities:
      - ALL
    allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
```

## Follow-up Questions
 - How would you extend the policy to cover init containers?
 - How would you test with a GitOps workflow and Gatekeeper audit pipeline?

Include a Gatekeeper or OPA policy, a minimal manifest that would be rejected (no egress gateway annotation or non allowlisted domain), a passing manifest, and steps for local and CI validation?
questions.answer: Design a Gatekeeper/OPA policy for a multi cloud Kubernetes platform that requires each Pod to include the annotation egress.gateway/enforced=true and to only allow outbound to an allowlisted domain l
questions.explanation:
## Why This Is Asked

Tests ability to design runtime security controls that span CI, policy engines, and multi cloud networking, ensuring outbound traffic is strictly controlled and auditable.

## Key Concepts

- Policy-as-code (Gatekeeper/OPA)
- Egress gateway design and TLS origination
- Outbound allowlist management
- CI validation for policy enforcement
- Cross-cloud consistency and testing

## Code Example

```yaml
# ConstraintTemplate for Gatekeeper enforcing egress gateway annotation
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8segressgateway
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sEgressPolicy
  targets:
  - target: obj  # pseudo example for clarity
    rego: |
      package k8segress
      default deny = true
      deny[msg] = msg {
        input.review.kind.kind == "Pod"
        not input.review.object.metadata.annotations["egress.gateway/enforced"] == "true"
        msg := "Pod must set egress.gateway/enforced=true to route via central gateway"
      }
```

```yaml
# Constraint demonstrating allowlist usage (illustrative)
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sEgressPolicy
metadata:
  name: require-egress-gateway
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
      - apiGroups: [""]
        kinds: ["Pod"]
  parameters:
    allowlist: ["app.allowed.example.com"]
```

```yaml
# Failing manifest (no egress annotation or disallowed destination)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: bad-egress-pod
  annotations:
    # missing or false
    egress.gateway/enforced: "false"
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:latest
```

```yaml
# Passing manifest (annotation set and allowlisted destination)
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: good-egress-pod
  annotations:
    egress.gateway/enforced: "true"
spec:
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginx:1.23.4
```

## Local and CI Validation

- Local: run gatekeeper locally to test ConstraintTemplate/Constraint; simulate Pod admission with k8s manifest.
- CI: run a workflow that lints manifests, validates policy via a test cluster or KinD, and fails on noncompliant manifests.
questions.diagram:
flowchart TD
  CI_Gate[CI Gate] --> P1[Policy: egress gateway annotation]
  P1 --> F1[Fail if missing]
  CI_Gate --> P2[Policy: allowlist destination]
  P2 --> F2[Fail if not allowlisted]
  CI_Gate --> A[Passing manifest detected]
